subtitle: Inner shin pain from overload

strava_risk_triggers.risk_note: Highest risk when weekly mileage jumps over 15% in one week

mild.description: Tenderness along inner shin, eases during run
mild.can_run_note: Yes — soft surfaces only, reduce pace
why: Bone stress response from repetitive impact on the tibia
Sudden surface change or mileage increase
relief: Ice shin 3 times daily for 15 min
Calf stretch against wall — 60s × 3 each leg
Switch to softer surfaces for 1–2 weeks
prevention: Eccentric calf raises on a step — 3 × 15 daily
Land midfoot, not heel — shorten stride slightly

moderate.description: Pain at the start and end of runs, tender to touch
moderate.can_run_note: Run/walk only on grass — 3 days rest between sessions
why: Tibialis posterior muscle fatigue contributing to overload
relief: Rest 3–5 days, switch to cycling or pool running
Ice massage with frozen paper cup along shin
Reduce weekly mileage by 50% on return
Consider compression socks for daily wear
prevention: Introduce strength work — tibialis raises and toe taps
Check foot strike pattern — video yourself running
Build mileage in 3-week cycles with a rest week every 4th

severe.description: Pain with walking, shin tender to touch, possible swelling
severe.can_run_note: Stop all running — rule out stress fracture first
why: Possible tibial stress fracture — needs imaging to rule out
Severe periosteal inflammation requiring proper rest
Bone remodelling unable to keep up with training load
relief: Complete rest from impact activity for 4–6 weeks
See a doctor — request X-ray or MRI to rule out fracture
Physiotherapy for load management and return plan
Pool running or cycling to maintain fitness
prevention: Never return to running through shin pain — it becomes a fracture
Structured return: walk → walk/run → easy run over 6 weeks
Address bone density if recurrent — calcium and vitamin D intake

see_physio_if: A specific spot that is very tender to press (possible stress fracture)
No improvement after 1 week of rest
